'' so get out a piece of paper and draw a coordinate system with `` actual voters '' in the center . and then throw a couple of darts at it to get a representative sample of `` likely voters . '' all pre-voting polls do just this . now , some polls target only a subsection of `` likely voters '' those you are calling `` biased . '' some , have low numbers or other problems , those you are calling poor quality , but no polls are centered on `` actual voters '' unless by pure chance your dart hit the center . the `` sampling '' error reported is about the dart hole . not about the position of the `` actual voter . '' so when you average the polls , what they are doing is converting this 2-dimensional sheet and aligning all the holes on top of each other . you can weight the sampling from that coninciding point all you want the actual error is still that whole sheet . beyond the `` likely '' vs. `` actual '' voter issues you have the cognitive bias of the `` survey effect . do n't need utility to talk about indifference curve . consider any bundle x. the set of bundles that you are indifferent to is the indifference curve ( note that it might not necessarily be a curve . for example , if preferences are not monotonic the `` curves '' could be thick ) . > is n't the whole basis behind rational actors that they are utility maximizing ? covered this already . utility comes from preferences , not the other way around . a common saying is `` preferences are the primitive '' . > i mean , you are not technically wrong , but to act like economics does not imply the favorability of certain policies i think is wrong . why would there be relative economic consensus behind , say , political candidates if the field did not lend itself to supporting certain policies ? the reason for which it was designed - was to target low- but fast flying aircraft . not to target slow-flying helicopters . as amazing as this might sound to a layman , that 's what different sa-8-operators serving in various countries ( including syria ) have told me . the system was simply developed to counter fighter-bombers , not really to counter attack helicopters . that 's how it works . that was not mentioned in this article - although conclusion was on hand , i.e . although it would be possible to 'connect the dots ' ( considering that majority of targets for jai 's sa-8s were assadist helicopters ) . means , the authors failed to research and find out one of reasons for the apparent 'lack of success ' of jai 's sa-8-operators . another issue not mentioned in that article is that the function of every single sa-8 vehicle is heavily dependent on a specific piece of equipment ( let 's call it that way in order to keep it simple for everybody ) - which has a relatively short operational life . means : in a situation like that in eastern ghouta - where insurgents are besieged since more than three years , and where one ca n't expect to get any spares - one ca n't run the system 'on ' for something like 24/7 . not even for 2 hours a day . one has to carefully gauge when to turn it on , and then quickly turn it off . it 's a matter of few minutes . obviously , under circumstances of prolonged siege and isolation from the outside world , primary issue is that of saving the machinery , so one can use it at another opportunity . but there is another aspect of operating in this fashion - and this is related to the very nature and sophistication of modern warfare . this aspect is : in order not to reveal own position . here we 're talking about literal 'seconds ' that make difference between life and death . namely , powering up the radar in the course of a modern-day war is like if you 're turning on a hand-lamp on a darkened stadium by night : everybody else on that stadium can see you , whether close by or far away ; but , you can see ... how many of others ? and everybody who can see you - can also kill you . means : powering up the sa-8 's radar means that the regime can detect emissions from that radar , and then - for example with help of triangulation - precisely establish its position . means : the moment the jai powers up the radar to guide its sa-8 missiles into their target -
